{
  "cells": [
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "# MDS5202 Assignment 1 (Python notebook)\n",
        "\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Conceptual Question 1\n",
        "- (a) Flexible method performs **better** because very large n and small p keep variance controlled while capturing structure.\n",
        "- (b) Flexible method performs **worse** because extremely large p with small n yields high variance and overfitting.\n",
        "- (c) Flexible method performs **better** since strong non-linearity needs adaptable models to reduce bias.\n",
        "- (d) Flexible method performs **worse** because very high error variance amplifies a flexible model's variance, harming test error."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "id": "8fc2000c",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Conceptual Question 3(a)\n",
        "Five curves on one plot (x-axis: flexibility increasing, y-axis: error):\n",
        "- Squared bias: high on the left, falls monotonically toward zero.\n",
        "- Variance: low on the left, rises monotonically.\n",
        "- Training error: drops monotonically with flexibility.\n",
        "- Test error: U-shaped: falls then rises as variance dominates.\n",
        "- Bayes/irreducible error: horizontal line above zero, constant."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "id": "b2542ce1",
      "metadata": {},
      "source": [
        "## Conceptual Question 3(b)\n",
        "- Bias drops because flexible models approximate the true function better.\n",
        "- Variance rises because flexible models react more to sample noise.\n",
        "- Training error drops as fit tightens.\n",
        "- Test error falls first (bias down) then rises (variance up).\n",
        "- Bayes error reflects noise and does not change with flexibility."
      ]
    },
    {

        "### Findings from College data\n",
        "- Private: 565; Public: 212. Private schools have higher out-of-state tuition (mean ~ 11,802) than public (~ 6,813).\n",
        "- Elite (Top10perc > 50): 78 vs 699. Elite schools show higher Outstate (mean ~ 15,249) than non-elite (~ 9,904).\n",
        "- Graduation rate: median ~ 65%, upper quartile ~ 78%.\n",
        "- Spending and tuition move together; Elite schools cluster at higher Expend and Outstate."
      ]
